topic_title: Recall — of all the things that were correct, how many did the model find

required_sections.detail: Sections 3, 4, 5, and 10 all present and non-trivial. Section 3 (Learning Objectives) ~94 words ≥ 50. Section 4 (Introduction) ~165 words ≥ 50. Section 5 (Core Concepts) ~1130 words across 7 subsections ≥ 100. Section 10 (Key Takeaways) ~115 words ≥ 50. No placeholder strings in required sections.

citation_density.citation_coverage_estimate: ≥70% of standalone factual assertions in sections 5, 6, 7 carry [N] markers. [1] appears in sections 4, 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 5.5, 5.6, 5.7, 7, 8. [2] and [3] also distributed throughout. A small number of explanatory/definitional sentences in section 5.4 are uncited but do not drop coverage below 60%.

findings.detail: 'ground truth' is used repeatedly (sections 5.2, 5.5, 5.6) as an assumed ML term. It does not appear in the prior-concepts allow-list for topics 7.1–7.8 or earlier weeks, and is not listed in 7.9's concepts_introduced. While context hints at its meaning in section 5.2 ('the item was actually positive in the real world (ground truth says yes)'), it is never explicitly defined as a term. Recommend adding a plain-English definition on first use or confirming it was introduced in an earlier topic not captured in the allow-list.

length_sanity.detail: Estimated ~2350 words. Falls within the standard corpus range of 1500–4000 words. Not more than 30% outside the range.

topic_scope.detail: No teaching of F1-score, ROC, AUC, specificity, or any metric beyond recall found. Section 5.7 covers the precision-recall trade-off, which is explicitly listed in 7.9's concepts_introduced and is needed to explain recall's denominator difference from precision. The last sentence of 5.7 properly names-and-defers any balancing metric ('covered in a later topic') without bolding or defining it. Precision calculations appear only as a comparison reference in section 5.5, which is appropriate scope.

findings.detail: 'ground truth' is used starting in section 5.2 without a plain-English definition on first use. The parenthetical '(ground truth says yes)' provides implicit context but does not define the term for a zero-background learner. Recommend inserting a one-clause definition, e.g., 'the ground truth — the actual correct label in the dataset'.

beginner_readability.terms_defined_on_first_use: All introduced terms (false negative, recall) are defined on first use with bold formatting and plain-English explanation. 'true negatives' is given an inline parenthetical that is sufficient.

soft_findings_summary.action_required: Add a plain-English definition of 'ground truth' on first use (section 5.2), or confirm the term was introduced in a prior topic.
Same as above — surfaces in both checks because it is both an unverified prior term and undefined on first use for a zero-background learner.

verdict: PASSED — all 6 hard-gate checks pass. 2 soft findings (both relating to the same term, 'ground truth', used without definition or prior introduction). Corpus is cleared for downstream artifact generation. Soft findings should be addressed before HITL #3 review.
